heroOverview: Pemphigoid is a group of autoimmune blistering disorders in which IgG autoantibodies target structural proteins of the dermal-epidermal junction, producing large, tense fluid-filled blisters on the skin and sometimes mucous membranes. Bullous pemphigoid — the most common subtype — has an estimated annual incidence of 21–66 cases per million people in Europe, with the rate doubling roughly every decade over age 70. Unlike pemphigus, the blisters form beneath the epidermis, so the skin above them stays intact longer and frank erosions are less immediate. The condition can be triggered or worsened by certain medications. Untreated pemphigoid carries meaningful morbidity and a mortality rate of approximately 10–20% in elderly patients within one year, primarily from infection and the side effects of required immunosuppressive therapy. With appropriate treatment, most patients achieve disease control.

keyStats.prevalence: Bullous pemphigoid incidence estimated at 21–66 per million per year in Europe; incidence has been rising, with some studies reporting a doubling of cases over recent decades
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly affects adults over 70 years; mean age of onset approximately 75–80 years; slightly more common in women than men
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age at diagnosis approximately 75–80 years; rare below age 60
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated tens of thousands of new cases annually worldwide; incidence data vary significantly by country and diagnostic criteria

definition: Pemphigoid (ICD-10: L12.9, unspecified) refers to a family of autoimmune subepidermal blistering diseases characterised by autoantibodies directed against hemidesmosomal and other junction proteins at the dermal-epidermal interface. The principal subtypes include bullous pemphigoid (BP, the most common), mucous membrane pemphigoid (MMP, also called cicatricial pemphigoid), pemphigoid gestationis (occurring in pregnancy), linear IgA bullous disease, and anti-p200 pemphigoid. In bullous pemphigoid — the prototype — IgG autoantibodies target BP180 (collagen XVII) and BP230, which are structural components of hemidesmosomes anchoring the epidermis to the dermis. Autoantibody binding activates complement and recruits eosinophils and neutrophils, which release proteases that cleave the lamina lucida and produce subepidermal cleavage. The resulting blister sits below the entire epidermis, explaining why the blister roof remains intact longer than in the intraepidermal blisters of pemphigus. Pemphigoid is managed by dermatology, often in consultation with internal medicine or geriatrics given the typical patient age and comorbidities. Pathophysiology involves both humoral and cellular adaptive immune mechanisms, and some cases are triggered by medications (dipeptidyl peptidase-4 inhibitors, checkpoint inhibitors, furosemide, spironolactone, penicillamine, and others).

typesClassification.description: The most common form, producing large tense blisters on erythematous or urticated skin predominantly on the trunk, limbs, and flexures; affects primarily the elderly; mucous membrane involvement is present in up to 20% of cases.

A chronic form targeting mucosal surfaces — particularly the oral cavity, conjunctiva, and genitalia — where scarring causes progressive destruction; ocular involvement can lead to blindness without aggressive treatment.

An autoimmune blistering eruption of pregnancy and the postpartum period, clinically similar to bullous pemphigoid, triggered by placental antigens; associated with a small risk of neonatal blistering.

An immune-mediated blistering condition with linear deposits of IgA (rather than IgG) at the dermal-epidermal junction; may be drug-triggered (especially vancomycin) and has a distinct 'cluster of jewels' blister arrangement.
A recently characterised subtype targeting laminin-γ1 (p200), often associated with psoriasis; clinically similar to bullous pemphigoid but identified by specific serological testing.

primarySymptoms: Large, tense, dome-shaped blisters (bullae) measuring 1–5 cm or more, filled with clear or slightly yellow fluid, most often arising on the trunk, inner arms, thighs, and groin.
Intensely itchy, red or urticarial plaques that appear weeks to months before frank blistering, representing the pre-bullous phase that is frequently misdiagnosed as eczema or urticaria.
Erosions and crusting at sites where blisters have ruptured, exposing a shallow red, weeping base that heals without scarring in most skin-limited disease.
Generalised pruritus (itching) that is often severe, disrupting sleep and daily activities, sometimes preceding visible blisters by months.
Redness and swelling of the skin surrounding the blisters (peri-bullous erythema), giving the lesion a bright inflammatory halo.
Oral blisters or erosions on the gums, palate, or inner cheeks in approximately 10–20% of patients, causing pain and difficulty eating.
Eosinophilia visible on a blood count in up to 50% of patients, reflecting the IgE- and IL-5-mediated eosinophilic component of the immune response.
Nikolsky sign is negative — unlike pemphigus, rubbing apparently normal skin adjacent to a blister does not produce a new erosion, because the blister roof is intact.
Secondary wound infection signs — increasing pain, purulent discharge, and surrounding warmth — when blister erosions become colonised with bacteria.
In mucous membrane pemphigoid: conjunctival redness, scarring, and symblepharon (adhesion of eyelid to eyeball) threatening vision if untreated.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent, intensely itchy red welts or hives that do not respond to antihistamines and last longer than typical urticaria.
Tense small blisters appearing on red skin, especially in the groin or axillae, in an older adult.
New oral erosions or blistering in an elderly patient without another explanation.
Itching that appears in late pregnancy without a prior skin condition, particularly beginning around the umbilicus.
emergencySigns: Widespread blistering covering a large body surface area with fever and signs of secondary infection (sepsis).
Rapidly progressive eye involvement in mucous membrane pemphigoid — redness, pain, discharge, or worsening vision — requiring same-day ophthalmology review.
Signs of corticosteroid toxicity (severe hypertension, confusion, high blood glucose) in a patient on high-dose systemic treatment.
Extensive mucosal involvement causing inability to swallow, risk of aspiration, or laryngeal involvement.

causes.cause: Autoimmune targeting of hemidesmosomal proteins BP180 and BP230
causes.description: In bullous pemphigoid, autoreactive T cells and B cells produce IgG antibodies against BP180 (the ectodomain of collagen XVII) and BP230; antibody binding triggers complement activation and eosinophil degranulation, releasing proteases that cleave the lamina lucida and cause subepidermal blister formation.

Numerous medications can trigger or exacerbate pemphigoid, most notably dipeptidyl peptidase-4 inhibitors (gliptins used for type 2 diabetes), checkpoint immunotherapy (pembrolizumab, nivolumab), furosemide, and spironolactone; drug-induced cases account for an estimated 5–15% of bullous pemphigoid diagnoses.

A striking epidemiological association exists between bullous pemphigoid and neurological conditions — Parkinson's disease, dementia, stroke, and multiple sclerosis — likely because neuronal isoforms of BP180 are expressed in the brain and may trigger cross-reactive autoimmunity after neurological injury.

The near-exclusive predilection of bullous pemphigoid for older adults reflects senescent shifts in immune regulation, including reduced T-regulatory cell function and altered B-cell tolerance, which allow autoreactive clones to escape suppression.

Specific HLA alleles — particularly HLA-DQB1*0301 — are significantly over-represented in bullous pemphigoid patients compared with controls, suggesting that antigen presentation molecules influence whether autoreactive immunity is triggered.

Sunburn, PUVA phototherapy, and radiation therapy have each been reported to trigger bullous pemphigoid in susceptible individuals, possibly by exposing or altering the antigenic structure of BP180 in the skin.

riskFactors.description: Incidence rises exponentially with age; the rate in those over 80 years is estimated at more than 200 per million per year in some European studies — approximately 300 times the rate in individuals under 60.

DPP-4 inhibitors (sitagliptin, saxagliptin, vildagliptin, linagliptin) are associated with a 2–4-fold increased risk of bullous pemphigoid; the mechanism may involve altered cleavage of BP180 at the skin surface. Risk is reversible on drug cessation.
Cancer immunotherapy agents including anti-PD-1 and anti-PD-L1 antibodies can unmask autoimmunity, including pemphigoid, in approximately 1–2% of treated patients.
Parkinson's disease, dementia, epilepsy, stroke, and multiple sclerosis each confer an approximately 2–6-fold higher risk of bullous pemphigoid compared with age-matched controls.
Patients with psoriasis have a higher incidence of bullous pemphigoid, partly because anti-psoriatic UV therapies may trigger it, and partly through shared autoimmune susceptibility.
Beyond the medication effect of DPP-4 inhibitors, diabetes itself may be an independent risk factor for pemphigoid, possibly through metabolic effects on skin barrier and immune function.

Women are slightly more frequently affected than men, consistent with the general female preponderance of autoimmune conditions, though the sex difference in pemphigoid is less pronounced than in many other autoimmune diseases.

affectedDemographics: Older adults aged 70 and over, the dominant group by a wide margin
Patients with Parkinson's disease, dementia, or a history of stroke
People with type 2 diabetes treated with DPP-4 inhibitors
Cancer patients receiving immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy
People with psoriasis or prior PUVA phototherapy
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of pemphigoid requires clinical recognition combined with histopathological and immunofluorescence confirmation. The clinical picture of large tense blisters on erythematous or normal skin in an older adult — particularly with a pre-bullous pruritic phase — is highly suggestive. A skin biopsy of a fresh blister for routine haematoxylin-eosin histology demonstrates subepidermal splitting with an eosinophil-rich infiltrate. Critically, a second biopsy from perilesional skin is sent for direct immunofluorescence (DIF), which shows a characteristic linear band of IgG and/or C3 at the dermal-epidermal junction in the lamina lucida in bullous pemphigoid. DIF is essential for diagnosis and distinguishes pemphigoid from pemphigus (which shows an intercellular fishnet pattern) and linear IgA disease (IgA rather than IgG). Indirect immunofluorescence on salt-split skin substrate further distinguishes subgroups. Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) for anti-BP180 and anti-BP230 antibodies are commercially available, with anti-BP180 ELISA sensitivity of approximately 85–90% for bullous pemphigoid; antibody titres can track disease activity during treatment. A complete blood count typically shows eosinophilia. Review of all current medications is mandatory to identify drug-induced cases; withdrawal of the offending drug can achieve remission without long-term immunosuppression in some patients. Mucous membrane involvement requires ophthalmology, oral surgery, and gynaecology input as appropriate to assess for scarring.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates subepidermal blistering with eosinophilic infiltrate, confirming the subepidermal split that characterises pemphigoid and distinguishing it from intraepidermal diseases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Under local anaesthesia, a small punch or shave biopsy of the edge of a blister is taken; the procedure takes under 10 minutes and leaves a minor wound.

diagnosticTests.test: Perilesional skin biopsy for direct immunofluorescence (DIF)
diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold-standard confirmatory test — detects linear IgG and C3 deposits at the dermal-epidermal junction in the lamina lucida, distinguishing bullous pemphigoid from other blistering diseases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A second biopsy from skin adjacent to (not inside) a blister is placed in a special transport medium and sent to an immunopathology laboratory; results take 3–5 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Serological detection of disease-specific IgG autoantibodies; anti-BP180 ELISA has approximately 85–90% sensitivity and can be used to monitor treatment response as titres fall with disease control.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw; results are usually available within 3–5 working days from a specialist laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Localises the immunoreactant to the epidermal (roof) side of the split in bullous pemphigoid, further characterising the autoantibody target and subtype.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on the serum blood sample, alongside ELISA; no additional patient procedure is required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies peripheral blood eosinophilia, present in up to 50% of bullous pemphigoid patients, supporting the diagnosis and reflecting disease activity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; result available within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies potentially causative drugs (DPP-4 inhibitors, checkpoint inhibitors, diuretics, penicillamine) that, if withdrawn, may induce remission without immunosuppression.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A systematic review of all current medications with the treating clinician, not a laboratory test.

treatmentOverview: Treatment aims to control blister formation, suppress the autoimmune response, allow healing of erosions, and achieve remission with the minimum long-term immunosuppressive burden necessary to keep disease controlled. For localised or mild disease, high-potency topical corticosteroids — specifically clobetasol propionate 0.05% cream applied to the whole body surface — have been shown in randomised trials to be as effective as oral prednisolone and safer in terms of mortality in elderly patients, making them the preferred first-line approach for non-extensive disease (Joly et al., NEJM 2002). Systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone 0.5–0.75 mg/kg/day) are used for extensive or rapidly progressing disease but carry significant toxicity risks in this age group, including diabetes, osteoporosis, cataracts, increased infection susceptibility, and adrenal suppression. Steroid-sparing agents — doxycycline, nicotinamide, dapsone, methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil, and azathioprine — are introduced early to allow prednisolone tapering and long-term disease control with lower cumulative steroid exposure. Disease severity and treatment response are monitored using the BPDAI (Bullous Pemphigoid Disease Area Index) and serial anti-BP180 ELISA titres. Rituximab, a B-cell-depleting anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody given as IV infusions, is increasingly used for cases refractory to conventional immunosuppression. Dupilumab, an IL-4/IL-13 antagonist, received NICE guidance in 2024 as a treatment option for bullous pemphigoid and is the first targeted biologic licensed for this condition in the UK. In drug-induced pemphigoid, early withdrawal of the causative medication — most commonly a DPP-4 inhibitor or checkpoint inhibitor — may achieve remission without prolonged immunosuppression, provided the underlying disease being treated can be managed with an alternative.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical clobetasol propionate 0.05%
medicalTreatments.description: High-potency topical corticosteroid applied over the entire skin surface; the preferred first-line treatment for non-extensive bullous pemphigoid based on randomised trials showing comparable or superior efficacy to oral prednisolone with lower systemic side effects in elderly patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Moderate disease controlled in approximately 80% of patients at 3 weeks in the landmark French RCT; 1-year mortality significantly lower compared with oral corticosteroids.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral prednisolone
medicalTreatments.description: Systemic corticosteroid (0.5–0.75 mg/kg/day) used for extensive or rapidly progressive disease, tapered gradually over 6–18 months as disease is controlled; carries significant risks of diabetes, infection, osteoporosis, and cardiovascular events in older patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves blister control in greater than 85% of patients but 1-year mortality in elderly patients with extensive disease treated with high-dose prednisolone approaches 30–40% in some studies due to treatment side effects.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline
medicalTreatments.description: An oral tetracycline antibiotic with anti-inflammatory properties beyond antibacterial action; used as a steroid-sparing agent either as monotherapy for mild disease or combined with nicotinamide; well tolerated in elderly patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: RCT evidence (UK BLISTER trial) shows doxycycline is non-inferior to prednisolone for blister control at 6 weeks with fewer serious adverse events; approximately 70–80% blister control rate.

medicalTreatments.name: Azathioprine or mycophenolate mofetil
medicalTreatments.description: Immunosuppressive agents used as steroid-sparing adjuncts to allow prednisolone tapering; azathioprine requires TPMT enzyme activity testing before use; mycophenolate mofetil is often preferred in patients with reduced TPMT activity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieve corticosteroid reduction and disease control in approximately 60–75% of patients when added to oral corticosteroids; response takes 4–8 weeks to become apparent.

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-CD20 B-cell depleting monoclonal antibody given as IV infusions; used for refractory bullous pemphigoid not responding to standard therapy; reduces autoantibody-producing B cells.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Published case series and prospective studies report complete remission in approximately 40–60% of refractory cases and partial remission in an additional 20–30%; duration of response typically 6–18 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Dapsone
medicalTreatments.description: An anti-inflammatory sulphone antibiotic that suppresses eosinophil function; useful as monotherapy for mild disease or combined with topical steroids in patients who cannot take systemic corticosteroids; requires G6PD testing before use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical improvement in approximately 60–70% of mild cases; less evidence from RCTs compared with corticosteroids.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nicotinamide (niacinamide)
alternativeTreatments.description: Combined with doxycycline or tetracycline as a non-immunosuppressive approach; anti-inflammatory via inhibition of mast cell degranulation; included in some treatment algorithms for mild disease.

alternativeTreatments.name: Wound care and emollients
alternativeTreatments.description: Regular application of emollients to non-blistered skin reduces itch and barrier disruption; antiseptic soaks or silver dressings on open erosions reduce secondary bacterial colonisation without systemic antibiotics.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist should be involved in any suspected pemphigoid to confirm the diagnosis with skin biopsy and immunofluorescence, select appropriate first-line therapy, and monitor for treatment toxicity. Mucous membrane pemphigoid requires coordinated multidisciplinary care with ophthalmology, oral surgery, and as needed otolaryngology, given the high risk of organ-threatening scarring. Refractory or rapidly progressive cases warrant discussion with centres experienced in rituximab or dupilumab use.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a dermatologist experienced in bullous disorders with access to in-house or rapid-turnaround direct immunofluorescence, anti-BP180 ELISA, and phototherapy if needed for co-existing conditions. For ocular involvement, simultaneous ophthalmology care is essential. Ask about the practice's experience with steroid-sparing regimens and whether they use disease activity scores such as BPDAI to guide tapering. Geriatricians or general physicians with dermatology experience are valuable given the typical patient age and comorbid conditions.
preventionStrategies: Review all medications with a prescriber if you are on DPP-4 inhibitors for diabetes and develop a new itchy skin rash — switching gliptin class may be considered.
Inform your oncologist immediately if a new blistering rash develops during checkpoint immunotherapy so the immune-related adverse event can be managed promptly.
Maintain good skin barrier function with daily emollients to reduce friction-related blistering in patients already diagnosed.
Wear loose, soft clothing to minimise mechanical friction on fragile skin during treatment.
Optimise management of co-existing conditions (Parkinson's, diabetes) to reduce systemic vulnerability to treatment side effects.
lifestyleModifications: Apply prescribed topical corticosteroids consistently as directed; skipping applications is the most common cause of treatment failure.
Avoid hot baths or showers that may rupture fragile blisters; use lukewarm water and pat skin dry gently.
Keep blister erosions covered with non-adherent dressings and change them daily using clean technique to prevent secondary infection.
Eat a high-protein, calcium- and vitamin D-rich diet to partially offset bone loss from systemic corticosteroids.
Report any new fever, wound redness, or worsening pain promptly, as secondary infection is the most common complication.
Limit sun exposure and apply SPF 50 sunscreen to areas affected by pemphigoid.

recommended: Adequate calcium (1,000–1,200 mg/day) and vitamin D (800–1,000 IU/day) supplementation for all patients on systemic corticosteroids to reduce osteoporosis risk
High-protein foods to support wound healing and offset the catabolic effect of corticosteroids
Potassium-rich foods (bananas, leafy greens) to counterbalance potassium loss from corticosteroids if applicable
Well-cooked, soft foods if oral erosions cause chewing pain
avoid: Alcohol, which increases the risk of osteoporosis and impairs immune regulation
Ibuprofen and NSAIDs without gastroprotection in patients on systemic corticosteroids, given the additive risk of gastrointestinal haemorrhage
Excess sodium, which worsens corticosteroid-induced fluid retention and hypertension

exerciseGuidelines: Gentle low-impact exercise such as walking or water aerobics is encouraged to maintain mobility and reduce steroid-induced muscle loss (sarcopenia) and osteoporosis. Patients with active blistering should avoid swimming in public pools until open erosions are closed. High-impact or contact activities that risk skin trauma are best avoided during active disease. Resume normal activity as lesions heal.
dailyManagement: Apply topical clobetasol or oral medications at the same time each day to maintain consistent drug levels.
Inspect the skin and any open erosions daily and note changes in blister numbers or wound appearance.
Keep a brief symptom diary to share with your dermatologist at follow-up — number of new blisters per day is the key metric.
Take prescribed bone-protection medication (bisphosphonate) and calcium/vitamin D if on long-term systemic steroids.
Attend follow-up blood tests as scheduled to monitor full blood count, glucose, and electrolytes during immunosuppressive therapy.
prognosis: Bullous pemphigoid typically has a remitting and relapsing course; approximately 30–40% of patients achieve complete remission off treatment within one year, and up to 50–60% by five years. Disease mortality is primarily driven not by blistering itself but by the age and frailty of the patient population and by the systemic side effects of corticosteroid treatment — particularly infection, cardiovascular events, and steroid-induced diabetes. In major cohort studies, 1-year mortality in patients aged over 80 approaches 20–30%. Drug-induced pemphigoid typically resolves within weeks to months of withdrawing the causative agent. Mucous membrane pemphigoid is more persistent and can cause irreversible scarring of the eyes and oropharynx without aggressive treatment. Recurrence after remission occurs in approximately 20–35% of patients; each recurrence is generally managed with the same approach as the initial episode.
recoveryTimeline: New blister formation typically slows within 2–3 weeks of starting effective treatment. Most blisters and erosions heal within 4–8 weeks of achieving adequate disease control. Tapering of corticosteroids typically begins after 2–3 months of full control and proceeds very slowly over 6–18 months to avoid relapse. Full discontinuation of treatment, when possible, is achieved on average at 12–24 months, though some patients require long-term low-dose therapy. Drug-induced pemphigoid may remit within 3–6 months of stopping the causative drug.
complications: Secondary bacterial infection of open blisters and erosions — wound colonisation with S. aureus or Pseudomonas is common and can progress to sepsis in immunosuppressed elderly patients.

Corticosteroid toxicity — long-term prednisolone causes steroid-induced diabetes, osteoporotic fractures, adrenal suppression, cataracts, glaucoma, and increased risk of serious infection, accounting for much of the condition's mortality.
Permanent scarring from mucous membrane pemphigoid — cicatricial scarring of the conjunctiva causes symblepharon and blindness, while oropharyngeal scarring can obstruct breathing or swallowing.
Relapse after treatment discontinuation — approximately 20–35% of patients in remission relapse, requiring retreatment.
Depression and anxiety — the chronic, disfiguring nature of the condition, combined with intense pruritus, significantly impairs quality of life in up to 40% of patients.
Anaemia from dapsone-induced haemolysis in patients treated with this agent, particularly those with low-normal G6PD activity.

supportResources.name: International Pemphigus & Pemphigoid Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient education, support groups, and clinician directory for pemphigus and pemphigoid conditions.

supportResources.name: NHS — Pemphigoid
supportResources.description: UK patient information on bullous pemphigoid symptoms, treatment, and when to seek help.

supportResources.name: British Association of Dermatologists — Bullous Pemphigoid Patient Information
supportResources.description: Plain-English leaflet on bullous pemphigoid diagnosis, treatment, and self-care from UK dermatology experts.

sources.title: Joly P et al. A Comparison of Oral and Topical Corticosteroids in Patients with Bullous Pemphigoid. NEJM 2002
Williams HC et al. Doxycycline versus Prednisolone as Initial Treatment for Bullous Pemphigoid (BLISTER). Lancet 2017
Murrell DF et al. Definitions and Outcome Measures for Bullous Pemphigoid: Recommendations by an International Panel of Experts. JAAD 2012
Pemphigoid — British Association of Dermatologists Guidelines (2012 update)
Kridin K et al. Epidemiology of Bullous Pemphigoid: A Systematic Review. Experimental Dermatology 2018
Marzano AV et al. Pemphigoid — Pathogenesis, Diagnosis and Treatment. NEJM Evidence 2022
NICE — Dupilumab for Treating Moderate to Severe Bullous Pemphigoid (NICE guidance, 2024)

confusedWithConditions.name: Pemphigus vulgaris
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pemphigus vulgaris produces flaccid, easily ruptured intraepidermal blisters with a positive Nikolsky sign, extensive oral erosions, and targets desmogleins; pemphigoid produces tense subepidermal blisters with a negative Nikolsky sign and targets hemidesmosomal proteins.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic urticaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic urticaria causes transient, non-blistering wheals and angioedema that resolve within 24 hours without scarring; pemphigoid in its pre-bullous phase causes persistent urticarial lesions that progress to blisters and is confirmed by skin biopsy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eczema
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eczema produces poorly demarcated, weeping, scaling plaques without tense blisters; the pre-bullous phase of pemphigoid is frequently mistaken for eczema, but DIF and serology distinguish them.

relatedConditions.name: Pemphigus vulgaris
relatedConditions.relevance: The main autoimmune blistering disease from which pemphigoid must be distinguished; different target antigens, blister level, and treatment approaches.

relatedConditions.name: Drug-induced pemphigus
relatedConditions.relevance: Drug-induced blistering that may overlap clinically; drug withdrawal is the treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Dermatomyositis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another autoimmune condition affecting skin in older adults; occasionally coexists with pemphigoid or is in the differential.

relatedConditions.name: Systemic lupus erythematosus
relatedConditions.relevance: SLE can cause bullous skin lesions through a subepidermal mechanism and enters the differential diagnosis in patients with multi-system autoimmune features.

faqs.question: What is pemphigoid and is it serious?
faqs.answer: Pemphigoid is an autoimmune condition where the immune system attacks proteins at the junction between the skin layers, producing large fluid-filled blisters. It is serious in that it is chronic and can cause infections and significant discomfort. In elderly patients, the 1-year mortality is approximately 10–20%, largely due to treatment side effects.

faqs.question: What is the difference between pemphigoid and pemphigus?
faqs.answer: Pemphigoid produces tense, hard-to-rupture blisters below the epidermis and a negative Nikolsky sign; rubbing normal skin does not peel it. Pemphigus makes fragile blisters within the epidermis and a positive Nikolsky sign. They target different skin proteins and are treated differently.

faqs.question: What causes pemphigoid?
faqs.answer: Pemphigoid is caused by the immune system making antibodies that attack proteins (BP180 and BP230) that anchor the skin layers together. This triggers inflammation that splits the skin and forms blisters. Certain medications — especially diabetes drugs called gliptins and cancer immunotherapy — can trigger it. Neurological disease and advanced age raise risk.

faqs.question: How is pemphigoid diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires a skin biopsy of a fresh blister (showing subepidermal splitting) and a second biopsy from adjacent skin for direct immunofluorescence, which shows a linear band of IgG along the skin's base. A blood test for anti-BP180 antibodies supports the diagnosis. These tests distinguish pemphigoid from similar conditions.

faqs.question: How is bullous pemphigoid treated?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment for moderate disease is a strong topical steroid cream (clobetasol) applied over the whole body surface, which is as effective as oral prednisolone and safer in older patients. For extensive disease, oral prednisolone is used. Steroid-sparing agents like doxycycline, azathioprine, or mycophenolate are added to reduce long-term steroid exposure.

faqs.question: Can pemphigoid go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Drug-induced pemphigoid may resolve within months after stopping the causative medication. Spontaneous pemphigoid rarely remits without treatment. With appropriate therapy, about 30–40% of patients achieve complete remission within one year and up to 60% by five years; some require long-term low-dose maintenance.

faqs.question: Is pemphigoid contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Pemphigoid is an autoimmune condition, not an infection. The blisters and skin lesions cannot be transmitted to other people through touch, skin contact, or any other means.

faqs.question: What medications can trigger pemphigoid?
faqs.answer: The most commonly implicated drugs are DPP-4 inhibitors (gliptins such as sitagliptin, linagliptin) used for type 2 diabetes, cancer immunotherapy checkpoint inhibitors, furosemide, spironolactone, and penicillamine. If pemphigoid develops while taking one of these, stopping it may allow remission without long-term immunosuppression.

faqs.question: Is pemphigoid painful?
faqs.answer: The blisters themselves can be uncomfortable, but the most prominent symptom is usually intense itching, which is often severe enough to disrupt sleep. When blisters rupture and form erosions, the raw skin can be painful, especially with movement or clothing contact. Oral involvement causes pain with eating.

faqs.question: Why does pemphigoid mainly affect elderly people?
faqs.answer: Pemphigoid incidence rises sharply with age — the rate in those over 80 is approximately 300 times that in people under 60. This is thought to reflect age-related changes in immune regulation that allow autoreactive antibodies to escape normal suppression, combined with the higher prevalence of neurological disease and DPP-4 inhibitor use in older adults.

faqs.question: Can pemphigoid affect the eyes?
faqs.answer: Yes. In mucous membrane pemphigoid (a subtype), the conjunctiva and eyelids are commonly affected. Scarring between the eyelids and eyeball (symblepharon) and corneal damage can occur and progress to blindness without aggressive treatment. Any eye symptoms — redness, discharge, pain, or blurred vision — need same-day ophthalmology review.

faqs.question: What is the diet advice for someone on steroids for pemphigoid?
faqs.answer: Take calcium (1,000–1,200 mg) and vitamin D (800–1,000 IU) daily to protect bones. Eat adequate protein to offset muscle loss. Reduce salt and refined sugar to limit fluid retention and blood glucose rise. Ask your clinician about a bisphosphonate for bone protection if you will be on prednisolone for more than 3 months.

faqs.question: Will pemphigoid blisters leave scars?
faqs.answer: Bullous pemphigoid blisters generally heal without scarring because they break below rather than within the epidermis. Mucous membrane pemphigoid is an important exception — scarring of mucous membranes is its defining feature and can cause permanent damage to the eyes, mouth, and throat.

faqs.question: How long will I need treatment for pemphigoid?
faqs.answer: Most patients need 1–2 years of treatment before remission is stable enough to attempt tapering and stopping. Some achieve full remission off treatment by two years; others need long-term low-dose maintenance. Relapses occur in about 20–35% after stopping, and retreatment with the original approach is usually effective.

faqs.question: Is pemphigoid related to pemphigus foliaceous?
faqs.answer: Pemphigus foliaceous is a separate autoimmune blistering disease that targets desmoglein-1 in the superficial epidermis, producing crusted erosions rather than tense blisters. It is distinct from pemphigoid in location, antibody target, and clinical appearance, though both are confirmed by immunofluorescence.

faqs.question: Can pemphigoid affect pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pemphigoid gestationis is a specific subtype that occurs during or after pregnancy when the immune system targets the same BP180 protein. It usually resolves after delivery but can briefly worsen at birth. Neonates occasionally develop mild blistering from maternal antibodies crossing the placenta. Specialist management throughout pregnancy is essential.

faqs.question: What blood tests are used to monitor pemphigoid during treatment?
faqs.answer: Anti-BP180 antibody titres by ELISA are measured every 2–3 months during treatment tapering; falling titres correlate with disease control. A full blood count monitors for steroid-related changes or dapsone-induced haemolysis. Blood glucose and bone profile are checked in patients on systemic corticosteroids.

faqs.question: Are there newer treatments for pemphigoid?
faqs.answer: Yes. Rituximab, a B-cell depleting biologic, is used for refractory cases with remission in 40–60% of treated patients. Dupilumab, which blocks the IL-4/IL-13 inflammatory pathway, has shown efficacy in clinical trials and received NICE guidance in 2024. Several other biologics targeting complement and type 2 inflammation are in development.

faqs.question: Does stress worsen pemphigoid?
faqs.answer: Physical or emotional stress can trigger flares of many autoimmune conditions including pemphigoid, though direct clinical data are limited. Managing stress through adequate rest and psychological support is a reasonable complement to medical treatment. Report any increase in blister numbers after periods of illness or stress to your dermatologist.

faqs.question: Can pemphigoid be associated with cancer?
faqs.answer: An association between bullous pemphigoid and internal malignancy has been investigated but the evidence is inconsistent; most large studies show no significant increase in cancer risk. Paraneoplastic pemphigoid is a distinct entity associated specifically with haematological malignancies, but standard bullous pemphigoid does not reliably warrant cancer screening beyond what is recommended for age.

metaDescription: Pemphigoid is an autoimmune skin disease causing large tense blisters in older adults. Learn causes, diagnosis, steroid and biologic treatments, and prognosis.
